content: July. Second full month of the summer internship, and it’s hitting its stride. The core feature I spent June on—the **'distance caching'** optimization—is live in production! I pulled up the dashboard this morning, and the performance metrics are showing a consistent **~25–30% speedup** on large route calculations compared to the old system. Seeing those graphs, knowing my code is *actually* saving the company time and compute resources... it's an incredible, tangible feeling of contribution. They've given me a new project now: researching potential **UI improvements** for the driver dispatch dashboard. They want me to look into modern React component libraries (MUI, Chakra UI, Radix UI, etc.) and maybe build some small prototypes to test user experience. It's less 'deep algorithm' work and more 'frontend architecture' and design, which is a nice change of pace. It feels like they trust my judgment and aesthetic sense, which is huge. This internship is exceeding all my expectations. Learning so much, contributing real value, and getting paid? The perfect summer trifecta.

Spent the 4th of July weekend with Maya. I drove up in my Civic! We went to a lake party with some of her high school friends. It was... fine. Different vibe than the Tech crowd—less talk about recursion and more about music. But Maya and I just stuck together mostly. We had another long, serious talk about the **'long distance'** reality looming over Sophomore year. It's scary. We're both nervous about how crushing the workload will be. Will we have time to call? Will we naturally drift apart as we make new friends? We reaffirmed our commitment to *try*. To communicate honestly, and to prioritize seeing each other during breaks, even if it's stressful. It's hard not having a 'guarantee,' but the genuine connection we share feels worth the uncertainty and the effort. It feels worth fighting for. Trying to stay optimistic, but the reality of 800 miles and 20 hours of academic workload is a daunting opponent.

Deep dive into **UI component libraries** for work. Built small, isolated prototypes using **Material UI (MUI)** and **Chakra UI**. MUI feels incredibly powerful and comprehensive, but maybe a bit 'heavy' in terms of dependencies and difficult to customize deeply. Chakra feels more modern, more 'developer-friendly' with its composition model (building complex components from simple, styled primitives). I wrote up a detailed comparison document for my mentor, outlining pros and cons, accessibility notes, and included working code sandbox examples. He was impressed with the thoroughness and our discussion about **'design systems'** and 'component consistency.' This frontend stuff isn't just 'making things pretty.' It's about 'user experience,' 'accessibility,' and 'maintainability.' It's *engineering*—just a different kind than the backend. I'm enjoying this holistic perspective shift.

Got my first *real* paycheck from the internship (bi-weekly pay structure). Seeing that number hit my bank account... wow. It's not 'Big Tech intern' money, but it's *mine*, earned through focused, *real work*. It makes the 'car fund' look much healthier, and makes the idea of **'financial independence'** feel less abstract and distant. College is expensive. Atlanta is expensive. I need to be smart about this. I started looking into budgeting and personal finance apps. Being a software engineer isn't just about writing good code; it's about managing your resources well. Adulting level 2 unlocked: **budgeting and saving**.

Spent all Saturday setting up the **SlatePath.ai** project structure for real. This is the big one. I initialized the Next.js (App Router) project in one repo (`SlatePath`). Set up **TypeScript**, **Tailwind CSS**, ESLint, and Prettier. Created a **Supabase** project in the cloud. Got the database connection string, set up the `.env` file securely. Defined the initial database schema using **Prisma** (`User`, `Course`, `Note`, `Flashcard` tables... keeping it simple for the **MVP**). Ran `prisma migrate dev`. The tables... exist! In the cloud! This feels real. Like laying the foundation for a skyscraper. It's just concrete right now, but the *potential*... it's exhilarating. This is *my* project. My vision. Let's build it.

Built the basic **'Auth' flow for SlatePath** today using **Supabase Auth**. Login page, Sign up page. I used the pre-built Supabase UI components (they're surprisingly nice and easy to integrate!). It just... works. Handles email/password, magic links, even Google OAuth. All in like... an hour. This is *insane*. Compared to the multi-day effort of building JWT auth from scratch in high school, this feels like cheating. But it's *smart* cheating. Why reinvent the wheel for a commodity feature like auth? Supabase is handling the boring, complex, security-critical stuff, so I can focus on the *interesting* stuff: the AI features. This decoupled, service-based architecture is definitely the way to go.

Last week of the internship. It's genuinely sad. I'm going to miss this team, the specific problem-solving, and the feeling of contributing to a real product. I gave my final presentation today on the UI library research and the prototypes I built. The CTO was there; he asked incredibly sharp questions about scalability and accessibility, but he seemed... genuinely impressed. My mentor gave me a glowing review, saying I exceeded expectations, learned incredibly fast, and made a real, quantifiable impact. He even said, **'We'd love to have you back next summer, maybe working on something even meatier.'** Wow. That's huge. A return offer on the table after just freshman year? That takes *so much pressure* off the 'Sophomore year internship hunt.' I need to process this amazing news.

Last day. Said my goodbyes to everyone. Exchanged contact info. It felt final, but also hopeful. Like closing one door and knowing another one (potentially the same one!) will open next year. This summer was truly **transformative**. Not just the skills learned (Postgres, Node, React architecture), but the *confidence* gained. I *can* do this. I *am* a software engineer, not just a 'student who codes.' That mental shift is everything. Headed home now, feeling proud, accomplished, and ready for the next chapter. (After a week of doing absolutely nothing, of course.)

This week is total **'limbo.'** Internship done, GT move-in is in 10 days. Just floating, trying to relax and 'be present.' But my brain won't turn off; it's already spinning. Thinking about SlatePath (I want to code!), thinking about the classes (1332!), and thinking about Maya leaving soon. It's hard to 'turn off' the engineering mindset. Spent today just driving around in my Civic, listening to music, no destination. Just clearing my head. Trying to appreciate this moment of quiet autonomy before the storm of Sophomore year hits.

Maya leaves for Michigan tomorrow morning. Tonight was our 'last date' for a while. We just went to our favorite local park and sat on a bench, talking for hours about nothing and everything. Trying to soak in every detail of her face, her laugh, and the familiarity. It was perfect, and heartbreaking. Saying goodbye *sucks*—there's no way around the pain. But we're committed. We have a routine plan. We have *us*. That has to be enough. It just *has* to be. I'm going to miss her like crazy.

Dropped her off at the airport this morning. Brutal. Came home. House feels empty and quiet. Okay. Need to channel this sad energy. Packing. Focus on packing. GT move-in is in 5 days. I need to get my stuff together. Boxes, labels, the whole chaotic routine. At least this year, I *know* what I need and what I *don't* need (mostly). It's slightly less chaotic than last year's panicked packing, but still stressful. Let the Sophomore year packing begin.

Car is packed. Tetris level: expert. Room is empty. Saying goodbye to my parents *again*. It's a little easier this time, more 'see you later,' less 'leaving the nest forever.' Still emotional, though. The drive tomorrow feels different. Not 'terrified freshman' energy. More... **'determined sophomore'** energy. I know what I'm driving *towards*: hard classes, cool projects, my friends, my independence. It feels good. Let's get this year started with confidence.

Back in Atlanta. Move-in day: Sophomore edition. Way, way smoother. I knew where to go and how to navigate the system. Unpacked in record time. My new dorm room is nice, maybe bigger, and hopefully on a quieter floor. My roommate seems cool (different one this year). It feels less like 'visiting' and more like **'coming home.'** Weird, huh? Atlanta is actually starting to feel like a second home. I didn't expect that emotional shift, but it's a good feeling.

First day of Sophomore Year classes. **CS 1332** (Data Structures & Algorithms)—yep, it's real. Professor seems intense. Syllabus looks terrifying (weekly homeworks *and* timed coding quizzes?). Diff Eq—already lost. Physics E&M—just gotta survive. I took a 'History of Architecture' elective for fun/humanities credit, and it might actually be interesting! The *vibe* on campus is familiar now, less overwhelming. I know where things are. I know people. It feels good. Less 'imposter,' more **'belonging.'** Okay. Year 2. Let's do this.

Started my campus jobs this week. First day at **OIT** (Office of Information Technology) as a Web Developer. Met the team; seems like a cool group. They have me working on maintaining some departmental WordPress sites for now. Basic stuff. PHP? Okay, a new language to vaguely learn. Not glamorous, but it's *real work*, good resume experience, and *paid*. Then, first meeting at the **ACT Lab** for Undergraduate Research. Met the professor and the grad students—super intimidating and brilliant. My first task? Read like five dense research papers on **'Sensor Fusion for Autonomous Vehicles.'** Okay. Deep end. Let's swim.

Okay. Juggling **CS 1332** homework (implementing an **AVL Tree**—kill me), OIT tasks (fixing a broken WordPress plugin), ACT Lab reading (what *is* a **Kalman Filter**?), *and* trying to have a semblance of a social life... is *hard*. Significantly harder than first semester. Sophomore year is *not* a joke. Sleep is already becoming optional. Coffee is mandatory. My respect for upperclassmen just went up exponentially. How do they *do* this for *four years* without total collapse?

The **AVL Tree** homework for 1332 nearly broke me. Rotations, balancing factors, keeping track of heights—it's *so easy* to mess up the pointers. I spent *hours* debugging, whiteboarding, and stepping through the code line by line. But when it finally *passed* all the tests—the *satisfaction*! It's immense. This class is brutal, but I'm *learning* deeply and fundamentally. This is the **core** of computer science, and it's worth the pain. (I hope.)

Labor Day weekend. Didn't go home. Just stayed here, trying to catch up on sleep, ACT Lab reading, and life. Had my first 'real' video call with Maya since we both got back to school. It was good. Needed. We're both swamped and stressed, but hearing her voice, seeing her face, it helps. It grounds me. We scheduled our next call. Making it a **routine** is the key to managing the distance. Consistency, even when it's hard.

My **OIT** work is okay. It's mostly frontend fixes—CSS tweaks, making sure things are responsive. It's not *challenging* technically, but it's **good practice** in attention to detail, working within an existing codebase, and following professional style guides. It's professional discipline. And the team is nice. My boss seems chill. It's a low-stress way to earn money and build the resume. Can't complain about the utility.

**ACT Lab** meeting. Discussed the papers I read. I tried to sound intelligent but mostly just nodded along while the grad students talked about 'probabilistic robotics' and 'state estimation.' My next task: Try to implement a simple **'Kalman Filter'** in Python to track a simulated object's position. Okay. *Now* we're talking. Code! Math! This feels more like my speed. Still intimidating, but *exciting*. This is **real research**, even if it's just a tiny, introductory piece.

CS 1332: **Heaps and Priority Queues**. Okay, this makes perfect sense. It's like a tree, but with specific ordering rules (min-heap, max-heap), making it highly efficient for finding the min/max element quickly. Used in event scheduling, graph algorithms (**Dijkstra's!**). Cool. Useful. The homework: Implement a MinHeap. Feels doable, compared to AVL trees, anyway. Maybe I'm actually getting the hang of this class and the data structures concept is starting to click.

Feeling **stretched thin**. The 'Sophomore Slump' might be real, or maybe just 'Sophomore Overload.' Classes are hard. OIT job takes time. ACT Lab requires *thinking*. Trying to see friends. Trying to call Maya. Trying to... sleep. And **SlatePath.ai** is just sitting there. I have the *idea* and the *Supabase* setup, but finding the *time* and the *energy* to actually *build* it is tough. Feeling a bit guilty, like I'm neglecting my 'passion project.' I need to find a way, even if it's just an hour a day. Maybe I can simplify the scope even further?

Started using my 'commute' time (walking between classes) to *think* about **SlatePath.ai architecture** and features. Just mental whiteboarding. Okay. Core **MVP**: Auth (done). Course creation. Basic note-taking. Flashcard creation (from notes). That's it. Keep it *simple*. For notes, maybe just **Markdown** support? Easy to implement. For flashcards, just front/back text? Yes. This feels achievable. Maybe I can build the 'Course' CRUD tonight? Just start somewhere, anything to get momentum back.

First **CS 1332 exam**. Covered everything up to AVL Trees. It was hard—long coding questions (implement a Queue using two Stacks!). Tricky Big O analysis. I *think* I did okay; it felt better than the Calc II midterm last semester. But this class is heavily curved, so 'okay' might still be a 'C.' Ugh. The stress. Just gotta wait for the grade and keep moving. Next up: HashMaps review and External Chaining.

October. Midterm season is *here*. Again. Diff Eq midterm next week. Physics midterm the week after. Plus a big 1332 homework due. And OIT deadlines. And ACT Lab progress expected. This is the **real Tech experience**. The 'drinking from a firehose' feeling is *back* with a vengeance. Just gotta keep swimming. Prioritize. Focus. Coffee. Lots of coffee.

Got my **1332 exam grade back. 88. An A!** *YES!* On the *first* notoriously hard 1332 exam! That is *huge*. The curve must have been generous, but still, an 88 is solid. Confidence *restored*. I *can* do this. I *understand* this material. This feels so good. Massive weight lifted. Okay. Now, focus on Diff Eq. Ugh.

Diff Eq midterm. It was... fine? Lots of 'solving homogeneous second-order linear ODEs.' It's purely **algorithmic**: follow the steps, find the roots, write the solution. Less 'conceptual' than Calc II. More procedural. I think I did okay. Maybe a B? That's all I need. Just survive the math classes by learning the system.

Saw a flyer: **'Reddit & Kiro Hackathon: Build AI Agents.'** Two weeks long. Remote. Interesting. Could this be a way to *force* myself to work on SlatePath? Frame the 'note-taking AI features' as an 'AI Agent'? Like, 'An agent that helps students learn by processing their notes'? It fits perfectly! And it's remote, so I can do it alongside schoolwork (ha!). Okay. Crazy idea? Maybe. But tempting. A deadline and a structure might be exactly what I need to get SlatePath off the ground. Signed up. Why not?

Hackathon kicked off today (virtually). The theme is broad: 'AI Agents for Good.' My **SlatePath.ai** idea fits perfectly. My goal for the next two weeks: Build the *backend* for SlatePath. Specifically, the API endpoints for notes/flashcards, and *one* AI feature. Maybe the **'note summarization' feature**? Using the OpenAI/Gemini API? Yes. That feels achievable. A concrete, deployable goal. Using the hackathon as my personal **'sprint.'** Okay. Game on.

First 'real' day working on the hackathon project (SlatePath backend). Decided to build the API using **Python/FastAPI**. Why? 1) Most AI/ML/LangChain stuff is in Python. 2) I want to keep the Next.js frontend 'clean'—just for UI and client-side logic. The Python backend will handle the 'heavy lifting' (AI calls, document processing later). It feels like a good **separation of concerns**—a 'decoupled architecture.' Spent today setting up the FastAPI project. Defining basic Pydantic models for 'Note' and 'Flashcard.' Building the first '/notes' CRUD endpoints. Connecting to Supabase (using the Python client). It's coming together rapidly.

Physics E&M midterm. It was **awful**. Even worse than I expected. Gauss's Law, Ampere's Law—I just didn't *get* it. Guessed on probably 70% of the problems. That's definitely a C, maybe lower. Ugh. It's official. I *hate* E&M; it's my **'kryptonite'** class. Okay. Deep breaths. It's *one class*. My CS grades are good, my research is good, my projects are good. This *one grade* won't kill me. It just *bruises* the ego a lot. Okay. Release the stress. Focus on things I'm *good* at: building this FastAPI backend.

Got the core CRUD API for Notes and Flashcards working in FastAPI. Create, Read, Update, Delete. Connected to Supabase. Tested with Thunder Client. It's *solid*. Now, the AI part. The **'summarization' endpoint**. Plan: Take a 'note ID' as input. Fetch the note content from Supabase. Send it to the **Gemini API** (using the Python SDK) with a prompt like 'Summarize this student's note concisely.' Get the summary back. I'll just return it for now to keep the MVP simple. Okay. Time to figure out the Gemini API...

Yesterday was intense. Got the **Gemini API integration working!** Built a `/notes/{note_id}/summarize` endpoint. It successfully fetches the note, calls Gemini (using the fast/cost-effective `gemini-1.5-flash-latest` model), and returns a stream of the summary. It feels **magical**. Like I just plugged a *real AI brain* into my application. This is the *power* I was looking for. This is **SlatePath.ai** coming *alive*. I spent way too late working on it, totally exhausted for my Friday classes. Also... saw another flyer. **Anthropic Hackathon.** *This weekend*? At Georgia Tech? In person? Focused on Claude 3.5 Sonnet? Am I insane? Should I do *two* hackathons at once during midterms? Probably not. But the *energy*. The *ideas*. The *people*. It's *so tempting*. I slept on it... and yeah. I'm doing it. Signed up this morning. This weekend is going to be pure, unadulterated chaos. And maybe... brilliant? Let's find out. Need coffee. Lots of coffee.
